coredata.dc:title: Discovering melamine-specific bioreceptors via phage display, constructing its validation method based on the quenching on nanocomplex, and applying screened bioreceptor to the electrochemical assay of melamine

coredata.dc:description: Blending melamine into foodstuffs is prohibited, but despite its harmfulness, melamine is still added to dairy products for falsifying nutritional composition. In this study, to discover the bioreceptor recognizing melamine, phage display was introduced. For the technique, two types of haptens and two kinds of phage display methods were used to improve its specificity through cross-checking each acquired result. The melamine hapten was synthesized by reacting 6-aminohexanoic acid with 2-chloro-4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazine, which function as a spacer and epitope site, respectively, and hapten was decorated on the bovine serum albumin (BSA) as a carrier material to manufacture BSA-hapten. Based on these haptens, the phage-displayed peptides are identified by sequencing, and five peptide bioreceptors were selected according to the frequency and similarity. To construct the simple verification procedure for the discovered peptides, graphene oxide quantum dot (GOQD)-Hg2+ quenching system was adopted, and their selectivity and sensitivity were determined. Briefly, the melamine-Hg2+ complex could easily be attached on the surface of GOQD through π-π stacking, thus, the fluorescence of GOQD is quenched via charge transfer. However, if the screened peptides possess an affinity to melamine, the diminished fluorescence intensity will be recovered after adding the bioreceptor to the system resulting from hindering the formation of the melamine-Hg2+ complex. We successfully figured out the valid peptide sequences among the candidates through this approach, and the best hit was applied to the electrochemical impedance spectroscopic system. As a result, it shows a high sensitivity to the melamine with a low detection limit.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the phage display method for BSA-hapten. Initially, a phage library is reacted with BSA, and the supernatant is collected, to eliminate the phages that have an affinity to BSA. Then, these BSA-negative phages are used in the phage display system of BSA-haptens.
Synthetic mechanism of (A) hapten and (B) BSA-hapten. (C) HPLC analysis of BSA and BSA-hapten. (D) MALDI-TOF spectra of BSA and BSA-hapten.

(A) Schematic illustration of a validation test for the candidates using a melamine-mediated GOQD-Hg2+ quenching reaction. (B) Relative fluorescence intensity of GOQD according to the correlations between the concentration of melamine and Hg2+ (λext =460 nm). (C) Relative fluorescence intensity of GOQD after adding the peptide (1.0 mg/mL) under optimized conditions [Melamine (0.13 mM) and Hg2+ (0. 31 mM)] (n = 3).
Quantitative calibration curve of melamine based on the inhibition of each candidate by the melamine mediated GOQD-Hg2+ quenching reaction. (A) Melp1, (B) Melp2, (C) Melp3, and (D) Melp5 (n = 5).
Selectivity test of each candidate to verify melamine specificity. (A) Melp1, (B) Melp2, (C) Melp3, and (D) Melp5 (n = 5).

(A) Schematic illustration of the electrochemical assay of melamine. (B) Optimization of the concentration of bioreceptor for melamine measurement. (C) The electrochemical assay of melamine under the optimized condition. (D) Selectivity test of the developed sensor with 1 ppm of melamine and other possible interferents. ΔRct is Rct (measured after reagent treatment) − Rct (peptide-modified SPE). (E) The electrochemical assay of melamine-spiked skim milk samples using the peptide-modified electrochemical biosensor (n = 3).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Blending melamine into foodstuffs is prohibited, but despite its harmfulness, melamine is still added to dairy products for falsifying nutritional composition. In this study, to discover the bioreceptor recognizing melamine, phage display was introduced. For the technique, two types of haptens and two kinds of phage display methods were used to improve its specificity through cross-checking each acquired result. The melamine hapten was synthesized by reacting 6-aminohexanoic acid with 2-chloro-4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazine, which function as a spacer and epitope site, respectively, and hapten was decorated on the bovine serum albumin (BSA) as a carrier material to manufacture BSA-hapten. Based on these haptens, the phage-displayed peptides are identified by sequencing, and five peptide bioreceptors were selected according to the frequency and similarity. To construct the simple verification procedure for the discovered peptides, graphene oxide quantum dot (GOQD)-Hg2+ quenching system was adopted, and their selectivity and sensitivity were determined. Briefly, the melamine-Hg2+ complex could easily be attached on the surface of GOQD through π-π stacking, thus, the fluorescence of GOQD is quenched via charge transfer. However, if the screened peptides possess an affinity to melamine, the diminished fluorescence intensity will be recovered after adding the bioreceptor to the system resulting from hindering the formation of the melamine-Hg2+ complex. We successfully figured out the valid peptide sequences among the candidates through this approach, and the best hit was applied to the electrochemical impedance spectroscopic system. As a result, it shows a high sensitivity to the melamine with a low detection limit.

ce:section.ce:para: Since outbreaking melamine incident in China, about 300,000 children in Shanghai who consumed the melamine-blended dairy products have suffered kidney and urinary problems, the issue in food safety has garnered great attention [1,2]. Melamine is a triazine containing three amino groups and has primarily been employed as an industrial chemical in the synthesis of melamine-formaldehyde resins, laminates, and plastics [3]. Nevertheless, melamine is deliberately added to dairy products to misrepresent their nutritional composition, because the quality of dairy products is evaluated via the Kjeldahl method possessing inherent weaknesses [4]. As this method only measures the total nitrogen content in the sample regardless of sources, this outcome is considered as an indicator of protein levels; hence, adding nitrogen-abundant melamine is of increasing the nitrogen content in foodstuffs with a low cost [5]. Melamine itself has low toxicity, but ingested melamine can be crystallized with cyanuric acid at the human renal system then induce acute nephrolithiasis and kidney stones [6,7]. Owing to its adverse effects, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) set a limit of melamine of < 1 ppm (8.0 μM) for infant milk, and 2.5 ppm (20 μM) for other dairy products [8]. Therefore, sensing melamine in foodstuffs is a momentous issue.

So far, there have been various attempts to detect melamine. Although instrumental analysis, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), gas chromatography, and Raman spectroscopy can identify even low levels of contamination, most share similar drawbacks, so on-site and real-time examination is relatively restricted [9–11]. These means require skilled personnel, are expensive, and take a relatively long time. As an alternative, colorimetric and fluorometric optical sensing systems have been designed. For colorimetric assay, melamine-mediated aggregation of gold or silver nanoparticles has been proposed [12,13]. Several kinds of nanomaterials showing fluorescence have been used to identify the presence of melamine, including carbon dots, upconversion nanoparticles, and quantum dots also. However, the sensitivity and selectivity of these methods are still not sufficiently high [14–17].

On the other hand, immunoassay technology is relatively sensitive and easy to manipulate, which has been widely used in screening harmful substances in food and the environment. Moreover, this way is particularly useful in analyzing foodstuffs having no fixed composition or form [18]. Although monoclonal antibodies for melamine have previously been studied, we focused on peptides to take advantages of its merits; their small size and high flexibility make them to effectively capture the small molecules like melamine, and they retain higher stability and lower manufacturing cost than antibodies [19–21]. Discovering the melamine-specific bioreceptor was conducted through the phage display, which has been used to develop bio-receptors for targets such as cell-surface proteins, chemical haptens, carbohydrates, and viral coat proteins [22–24]. This technique can be applied to myriad fields, including diagnostics, therapeutics, and basic research, owing to its cost-effectiveness, time efficiency, and high sensitivity and selectivity [25,26]. For the phage display, two types of hapten were prepared, and these haptens were screened using two kinds of phage display methods to enhance the specificity of obtained bioreceptor by cross-checking each result. To simplify the validation process of the discovered candidates, we designed a melamine-mediated graphene oxide quantum dot (GOQD)-Hg2+ quenching system. Furthermore, the best candidate selected by the developed approach was applied to the electrochemical biosensing system. Since electrochemical sensors are relatively easy to be applied to the portable device system as well as simple in operation and sensitive to the target [27,28].

Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4), isopropyl β-d-thiogalactopyranoside (IPTG), melamine, 5-bromo-4-chloro-3-indolyl β-d-galactoside (X-Gal), sodium chloride (NaCl), bromophenol blue, sodium bicarbonate, tetramycin, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), xylene, o-dichlorobenzenes, bovine serum albumin (BSA), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), trichloroacetic acid (TCA), toluene, and phenol were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), 6-aminohexanoic acid (AHA), polyethylene glycol (MW: 8000), trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), acetic acid, and sodium iodide were purchased from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA, USA). 2-chloro-4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazine (CAAT) was purchased from TCI (Tokyo, Japan). Bacto tryptone, yeast extract, and skim milk were purchased from BD Bioscience (San Jose, CA, USA). Graphite nanoparticles (GNPs, average diameter: 3−4 nm, 93 %) were purchased from Skyspring Nanomaterials (Houston, TX, USA). The SMART™ BCA Protein Assay Kit was purchased from iNtRON Biotechnology (Seongnam, Korea). Agarose was purchased from Takara (Shiga, Japan). Sucrose, glycine, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), and sulfuric acid were purchased from Junsei (Tokyo, Japan). The linear heptapeptide (Ph.D.-7) and dodecapeptide (Ph.D.-12) phage display library kits were purchased from New England Biolabs (Ipswich, MA, USA). M13 bacteriophage antibody (HRP conjugate) and mouse mAb were purchased from GE Healthcare (Chicago, IL, USA). Methanol (MeOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), and ethanol (EtOH) were purchased from Merck (Kenilworth, NJ, USA). Mercury (II) chloride, dichloromethane (MC), and nitric acid were purchased from Daejung-Chem (Siheung, Korea). HPLC-grade deionized (DI) water and acetonitrile (ACN) were purchased from Duksan (Seoul, Korea). Coomassie blue and tris were purchased from Bio-Rad (Hercules, CA, USA). N, N’-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) was purchased from WAKO (Richmond, VA, USA). All reagents were used as received, and DI water prepared using the Direct-Q® Water Purification System (Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA) was used for the whole experiment.

Fluorescence intensity was measured using a multi-mode microplate reader (BioTek Synergy H1, Winooski, VT, USA). Nuclear magnetic resonance spectra (NMR) were recorded on a Varian Unity VNS 600 NMR spectrometer (1H and 13C at 600 MHz, Varian, Walnut Creek, CA, USA). Gas chromatography/high-resolution mass spectrometry (GC/HRMS) was carried out using an MS-700 (JEOL, Akishima, Japan) mounted with a gas chromatograph (6890 N, Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) ranging from 4000 to 500 cm−1 was recorded using an FTIR-6600-FV spectrometer (Jasco, Tokyo, Japan), and samples were dispersed in KBr pellets before analysis. BSA-hapten was identified by matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization time of flight (MALDI-TOF) mass spectrometry on Voyager DE-STR (Applied Biosystems, Foster City, CA, USA) and HPLC (Agilent 1260) equipped with a Nova-Pak C18 column (3.9 mm i.d. × 15 cm, 4 μm; Waters, Milford, MA, USA). Gel images were taken using a ChemiDoc™ MP Imaging System (Bio-Rad, Hemel Hempstead, UK). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of GOQD were obtained using a Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN instrument from FEI (Hillsboro, OR, USA). The data processing and representation was conducted using Origin 2018 software (OriginLab, Northampton, MA, USA).

Fig. 1 illustrates the phage display procedure using BSA-hapten to enrich the positive hits of affinity peptides toward melamine. As the first step for acquiring BSA-hapten, CAAT-AHA (hapten) was prepared. Typically, 50 mL of 0.27 M NaOH was injected dropwise at 85 °C into a solution containing CAAT (1.0 g, 6.87 mmol) and AHA (1.08 g, 8.24 mmol) in 20 mL DI water. The reaction was carried out under reflux conditions for 17 h with stirring. The solution was filtered to remove the white solid of unreacted substrate, and 6 M HCl was added to the filtrate to bring it to pH 3.0. The filtrate was extracted with MC (20 mL × 3), then the organic layer was concentrated and dried at 60 °C for 6 h. The generated white solid was washed with DI water and dried under the same conditions. The obtained hapten was a white solid with a yield of 62.1 %. TLC Rf = 0.2 (MC:MeOH = 3:1, v/v). GC/HRMS (EI) m/z calculated for C9H17N6O2 [M+H] 241.1412, found 241.1413. 1H NMR (DMSO-d6 , 600 MHz) δ: 1.24–1.30 (m, 2 H), 1.40–1.56 (m, 4 H), 2.20 (t, J =7.23 Hz, 2 H), 3.16 (q, J =6.71 Hz, 2 H); 5.95–6.20 (br, 4 H), 6.46 (t, J =5.93 Hz, 1 H). 13C NMR (DMSO-d6 , 600 MHz) δ: 175.14 (C-1), 40.20 (C-6), 34.20 (C-2), 29.57 (C-5), 26.47 (C-4), 24.80 (C-3). IR (KBr) max (cm−1): 3460 (s), 3335 (s), 3200 (s), 2941 (s), 2869 (w), 1672 (s), 1593 (vs), 1500 (s), 1474 (s), 1458 (s), 1400 (s), 1374 (s), 1311 (s), 1244 (w), 1206 (w), 1166 (w), 1001 (w), 920 (w), 791 (s), 738 (w), 699 (w), 603 (w), 572 (w). The recorded spectra of 1H, 13C NMR, and IR results for hapten are shown in Figs. S1A-B and S2, respectively.

To decorate hapten on the BSA, the hapten (20.0 mg, 0.083 mmol) was dissolved in 1 mL DMF, and NHS (14.0 mg, 0.125 mmol) was transferred to the solution at 4 °C. After stirring for 10 min, DCC (26.0 mg, 0.125 mmol) was added. The blended solution was refrigerated to activate hapten with NHS at 4 °C for 15 h. Thereafter, it was filtered to remove dicyclohexylurea and unwanted reactants. The filtrate was added dropwise with stirring to a solution of BSA (28 mg) in DI water (2 mL) and incubated for 15 h. The mixture was centrifuged at 15,814×g for 10 min, and the acquired supernatant was then dialyzed for three days (MWCO = 3000).

BSA-hapten was verified by agarose gel electrophoresis, HPLC, and MALDI-TOF analysis. For the electrophoresis, a non-denaturing agarose gel electrophoresis method was followed [29]. Briefly, a gel loading buffer was prepared by mixing 0.04 % (w/v) bromophenol blue and 7 % (w/v) sucrose. BSA and BSA-hapten of 10 μL (5.0 mg/mL) were mixed with a 10 μL gel loading buffer, respectively, and each sample was separated at high voltage for 30 min. The gel was immersed in 20 % (v/v) TCA solution for 1 h and then stained with Coomassie blue for 1 h. The stained gel was immersed in a destaining buffer (250 mL EtOH, 80 mL acetic acid, 670 mL DI water) for 12 h.

For the HPLC analysis, BSA and BSA-hapten were pre-filtered through a 0.2-micron syringe filter. Mobile phase A was 10 % (v/v) ACN in DI water containing 0.1 % (v/v) TFA, and mobile phase B was 90 % (v/v) ACN in DI water containing 0.1 % (v/v) TFA. The flow rate was 1.0 mL/min with a linear gradient from mobile phase A to mobile phase B for 1 h, and the column temperature was 30 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phage display of the BSA-hapten plate
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to the phage display, BSA and BSA-hapten were diluted to 100 μg/mL with 0.1 M NaHCO3 (pH 8.6) and treated in a 96-well plate (SPL, Seoul, Korea) for 20 h at 4 °C. The plate was blocked with phage blocking buffer (0.1 M NaHCO3; pH 8.6, 5 mg/mL BSA, filter sterilized), then washed with TBST buffer (20 mM Tris; pH 7.5, 150 mM NaCl, 0.1 % (v/v) Tween 20, filter sterilized). Next, 1 × 1011 pfu/mL of a phage library in 100 μL of TBST was incubated for 1 h at 25 °C in the BSA-containing wells. After 1 h, the phage library was transferred to the BSA-hapten wells and incubated for 1 h at 25 °C. Each well was washed out with TBST and eluted with 100 μL glycine-HCl (0.2 M; pH 2.2) for 20 min at 25 °C. Finally, 15 μL of Tris−HCl (1 M; pH 9.1) was added to the eluent, and the mixture was transferred to a clean tube.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization of hapten on the plate (hapten-linked plate) and its phage display
ce:section.ce:para: To graft the amino groups on the surface of 12-well plate (SPL, Seoul, Korea), it was treated with O2 plasma for 3 min with an O2 plasma system (CUTE-MP, Femto Science, Hwaseong, Korea), 500 μL APTES (0.5 % (v/v) in DI water) was then cast on each well. After 5 min, the plate was washed with DI water five times. Simultaneously, hapten (10.0 mg, 0.04 mmol) was dissolved in 5 mL DMF, and EDC (9.6 mg, 0.05 mmol) and NHS (5.7 mg, 0.05 mmol) were successively added to the mixture. NHS-activated hapten was dropped on the well at 25 °C and allowed to react for 1 h. After incubation, the plate was washed with DI water five times and stored at 4 °C.

The hapten-linked plate was blocked with a phage blocking buffer. After the plate was washed with TBST, it was treated with 1 × 1011 pfu/mL of a phage library in 500 μL TBST for 1 h at 25 °C. After discarding the unbound phage library with TBST, potential candidates stuck on the hapten were eluted with 500 μL glycine-HCl (0.2 M; pH 2.2) for 20 min at 25 °C. Finally, 15 μL of Tris−HCl (1 M; pH 9.1) was added to the eluent for neutralization, and the mixture was transferred to a clean tube.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide quantum dot
ce:section.ce:para: GOQD was prepared from GNPs powder [30]. The pristine GOQD solution was obtained by sonicating 1.0 g GNPs in 80 mL of oxidizing solution (H2SO4:HNO3 = 3:1, v/v). The mixture was stirred for 12 h at 100 °C with a reflux system then cooled to room temperature. The solution was placed in the ice bath, and 50 mL DI water was added. After gathering the supernatant through the centrifugation at 4585×g for 20 min, the pH was adjusted to 8.0 using 5.0 M NaOH solution. Finally, salts and impurities were removed by dialysis (MWCO: 1000 Da) for seven days. The size of the GOQD was estimated using calibrated TEM images and Gatan Digital Micrograph™ software (Gatan, Pleasanton, CA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the melamine-mediated GOQD-Hg2+ quenching reaction and peptide candidate verification
ce:section.ce:para: Like as the fluorescence intensity of graphene quantum dot (GQD) is quenched by melamine-Hg2+ complex in previous work [31], we discovered that GOQD also demonstrates similar behavior of GQD. The result inspired an opportunity to devise a simple way to verify the performance of the bioreceptor. To construct the optimized conditions, GOQD (0.1 mg/mL), different concentration of Hg2+ (0–1.25 mM), and melamine (0–2.0 mM) were prepared. After mixing 50 μL of each species and adding DI water until the final volume of 200 μL, fluorescence was measured using a multi-mode microplate reader. The validity of peptide candidates was determined by adding peptide (1.0 mg/mL) instead of 50 μL DI to the optimized conditions then comparing fluorescence intensity with the negative control. For the selectivity test, 0.75 mM of hapten and aromatic reagents (Xylene, toluene, o-dichlorobenzenes, and phenol) were prepared and applied to this system in place of melamine.

The preparation of melamine-spiked sample was carried out by the described procedure in the literature with slight modifications [32]. Briefly, melamine (2 mg) was dissolved in 10 mL of 61 mM TCA and 5 mL ACN, and it was blended with 5 mL of skim milk solution (0.2 g/mL). The mixture was sonicated for 15 min and centrifuged at 10,000×g for 10 min. The supernatant was transferred to a clean tube and adjusted to pH 7.0 with 1.0 M NaOH. The solution was centrifuged at 10,000×g for 10 min, and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.45-micron filter membrane. A pretreatment milk sample that did not include melamine was prepared in the same way as the melamine-spiked sample. The spiked milk sample was diluted with the pretreatment milk sample, then subjected to the optimized GOQD quenching system.

The measurement of melamine was achieved by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique utilizing the melamine-specific capturable peptides, which was screened by melamine-mediated GOQD-Hg2+ method. In this case, thiol residue was attached to the C-terminal of Melp2 to facilitate the electrode modification via Au-S interaction. To optimize the assay conditions, 15 μL of various concentrations of Melp2 (0.05–1.0 mg/mL) were cast on the working electrode region (4.0 mm diameter, area 12.6 mm2) of Au screen-printed electrode (Au-SPE) (C220AT, EG Technology, Seoul, Korea) then dried for 1 h at 4 °C. After the electrode was washed with DI for 3 times, EIS was monitored by a CHI 750E instrument (CH Instruments, Austin, TX, USA) connected to a computer data analysis system, and Randles equivalent circuit was adopted as a model (Fig. S3). The electrochemical responses were measured in a PBS solution (10 mM; pH 7.2) supplemented with 25 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, and EIS was recorded under AC potential in the range of 0.09–0.13 V determined by open circuit potential through frequency from 100 kHz to 1 Hz. For the sensitivity test for melamine, 15 μL of different concentrations of melamine (0.01–1.0 ppm) was dropped on the bioreceptor-immobilized Au-SPE then incubated for 1 h at 4 °C. The unreacted reagents were removed by DI for 3 times, and EIS of each electrode was recorded following the above procedure.

Designing and characterizing a suitable hapten for phage display is directly connected to the specificity and selectivity of phage-displayed peptides toward the target [33,34]. As shown in Fig. 1, this experiment begins with immobilizing the target substance on the plate. Melamine has three aromatic amine groups, but using these intrinsic groups hold problems. Firstly, the reactivity of aromatic amines is less than that of primary amines; therefore, anchoring melamine on the plate is difficult [35]. Even if melamine is fixed to the plate, the same reactivity of these aromatic amines in melamine because the symmetry prohibits the orientation control of epitope site. For these reasons, melamine was haptenized by a nucleophilic aromatic substitution reaction. Starting from CAAT acting as an epitope of melamine, AHA possessing the optimal chain length to screen bioreceptors was reacted under basic conditions [36]. The synthetic mechanism for hapten is shown in Fig. 2 A.

In some cases, hapten with low molecular weight is conjugated to other carrier molecules, such as BSA or ovalbumin, to efficiently expose its epitope and enhance the immune response [37]. The prepared hapten is immobilized on BSA (BSA-hapten) by DCC/NHS coupling (Fig. 2B). There are lysine residues bearing amine groups in BSA, and that provide places for hapten immobilization. The identification of BSA-hapten was carried out using non-denaturing agarose gel electrophoresis, HPLC, and MALDI-TOF. Non-denaturing agarose gel electrophoresis is a useful method for distinguishing whether haptens are conjugated on the protein because the conjugation of even two small molecules induces diverting the net charge of the protein [38]. The image of the agarose gel in Fig. S4 shows that the BSA-hapten band is blunter and located upper than that of BSA owing to the increased molecular weight by linked hapten. Although enough amount of hapten was adopted to the reaction, it is difficult to fully react with the hapten with all the amine groups on BSA; thus, the BSA-hapten comprised of various mixtures. Following the HPLC conditions reported elsewhere [39], BSA and BSA-hapten were analyzed by reverse-phase HPLC, which separates the samples by their hydrophobicity. While BSA was eluted with a single sharp peak at 28.9 min, the retention time of the BSA-hapten was delayed to 31.0 min with a broad peak resulting from its changed polarity (Fig. 2C). With the application of hydrophobic hepten to BSA, the polarity of BSA is reduced and the propagation speed of BSA-hepten is slower due to the polarity similar to column in separation. To investigate how many haptens are immobilized on the BSA-hapten, MALDI-TOF was implemented (Fig. 2D). Whereas the molecular weight (MW) of BSA is 66.2 kDa, which is well-matched with the original MW of BSA, the main peak of the BSA-hapten was observed at 71.9 kDa. The number of haptens conjugated to BSA can be calculated by subtracting each MW, and it was figured out that the BSA-hapten was successfully synthesized, and approximately 23.8 counts of hapten were anchored on the BSA surface.

The property of GOQD was characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy and TEM imaging. The FT-IR spectra of GOQD are shown in Fig. S5. Since GOQD is prepared by oxidizing GNPs, a band at 3600–3200 cm−1 (OH stretching) and near 1725 cm −1 (CO) appeared. Moreover, a strong peak at 1100 cm−1 is due to the presence of CO related groups such as ester, epoxide, hydroxyl, and ether [40]. The TEM image of GOQD (Fig. S6) represents its morphology and size, and GOQD had an average diameter of 2.1 nm with a narrow size distribution from 1 to 3 nm. Based on the above results, it was revealed that GOQD is successfully synthesized from GNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phage display of the hapten-linked plates and BSA-hapten immobilized plates
ce:section.ce:para: The phage display of hapten was followed by conventional procedure except for the way of hapten fixation on the plate [22–24]. Considering that size of the hapten is too small to stimulate the animal immune response, it was decorated on the macromolecular carriers, BSA [41]. Moreover, unlike antibody production using living organisms, it is also available directly employing hapten to discover the bioreceptor as a phage display proceeded with a confined set of pools. For hapten fixation on the support, a six-well plate was pretreated with O2 plasma to generate radicals on the surface and then reacted with APTES to graft amine groups. After immobilization of the hapten on the plate via EDC/NHS coupling, the phage display of the hapten-linked plate was implemented (Fig. S7). On the contrary, the phage display of BSA-hapten is sequentially proceeded by two steps: removing BSA-positive phage is first, then BSA-hapten positive phage display (Fig. 1). The reason for the former step is that unwanted phage libraries could remain after panning, in that the size of the peptides is smaller than the antibody [42]. Thus, the phage library was firstly reacted with BSA to reduce nonspecific sequences, and then the supernatant containing the BSA-negative phage library was applied to the phage display of BSA-hapten to enhance its specificity.

The phage display of hapten and BSA-hapten was conducted until the third panning, and the phage recovery rate had increased according to the number of cycles (Table S1). After the experiment, five to seven individual plaques were collected from each sample, and single-stranded DNA reflecting innate information of the candidate peptide was extracted from the phages. Table S2 shows the peptide sequence derived from the single plaque of the phage, and we found that some sequences present remarkable similarities. Especially, those beginning with ATP- or FYSH- and ending with -SHRSISS were noticed to occur on both hapten-linked and BSA-hapten plates, regardless of the length of the peptide library. Five candidates that appeared frequently and overlapped sequences were selected to synthesize, and their repeated sequences to verify their melamine affinity (Table S3). These results indicate that the additional process, removing BSA-positive peptide library, helps to discover the target-specific bioreceptors, effectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the peptide candidates through a melamine-mediated GOQD-Hg2+ quenching reaction
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to developing the impedimetric sensors for melamine, a validation method for the obtained candidates has been established using GOQD-Hg2+ quenching system. This proposed system diminishes the ineffective step of investigating the unproven peptides because modifying those peptides on the SPE requires tedious processes of optimizations. To simplify the validation process of the discovered bioreceptors, a melamine-mediated quenching effect between GQD and Hg2+ was introduced with modifications as a previous report [31]. Through a series of experiments, it was turned out that GOQD exhibits the analogous effect with GQD, and this behavior can be utilized to construct the peptide validation method. GOQD is a carbon-based nanomaterial with a fluorescence signal (λext =460 nm, λemi =535 nm), and various oxygen-related functional groups in GOQD improve the water solubility more than that of GQD [43]. The GOQD-Hg2+ quenching reaction is based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer. This phenomenon is highly affected by the distance between the donor-accepter pair, and it is generally known that an effective distance of 1–10 nm [44]. As the affinity between GOQD (donor) and Hg2+ (acceptor) is weak, the quenching reaction of GOQD does not take place unless GOQD and Hg2+ exceed the certain concentrations. However, melamine could bind to not only Hg2+ ions, as forming the melamine-Hg2+ complex, but also GOQD via π-π interaction. Therefore, if the screened peptides have an affinity to melamine, the diminished fluorescence intensity will be recovered by hindering the formation of the melamine-Hg2+ complex. The schematic diagram of this mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 3 A.

Before establishing the validation methods for peptide, GOQD-Hg2+ system was tracked under different amounts of Hg2+ and melamine to find out the optimal Hg2+ concentration. As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence intensity of GOQD decreased as the concentration of Hg2+ and melamine increased, respectively. Whereas fluorescence intensity was gradually descended according to the melamine level at 0.31 mM and 0.63 mM of Hg2+, no discernible tendency was observed with respect to the concentration of melamine and mercury for the other conditions. Thus, 0.31 mM Hg2+ was selected for minimizing the influence of Hg2+. Next, various peptides, including the candidates and non-related peptides, were subjected to the system to verify this strategy under conditions of 0.13 mM of melamine and 0.31 mM of Hg2+. The y-axis in Fig. 3C represents F-F0 (RFU), where F0 is the fluorescence intensity before adding the peptide and F is fluorescence intensity at that time from injecting the peptide. After treating the peptide to the quenched GOQD, almost fluorescence intensities of the candidates increased until 60 min except for Melp4. After optimizing the quantitation conditions, each step was specifically investigated by fluorescence spectroscopy with Melp2 (Fig. S8). While Hg2+ itself could slightly reduce the fluorescence intensity of GOQD, the intensity was dramatically decreased after melamine is added by forming melamine-Hg2+ complex. The quenched fluorescence of GOQD is recovered as adding the Melp2 lastly, and it was observed that significant intensity enhancement (Fig. S9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applying the validation system for the melamine sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To figure out whether this developed system has a potential as the optical sensors for melamine, the fluorescence intensity was measured with various concentrations of melamine (1–100 ppm) (Fig. 4 ). The groups with proven their performance (Melp1, Melp2, Melp3, and Melp5) demonstrated a tendency to recover fluorescence with increasing melamine concentrations and had a limit of detection (LOD) of 9.96, 3.12, 18.37, and 10.33 ppm, respectively. Although these detection limits are slightly higher than that of FDA regulations, this approach has meaningful and useful as the simple validating means for the phage-displayed peptide. Moreover, the obtained sequences majorly contain aromatic amino acids, such as phenylalanine and tyrosine. According to a previous report, those kinds of amino acids can interact with graphene oxide (GO) and induce a quenching effect [45]. In this study, we are using GOQD and the only difference between GO and GOQD is in terms of their size. Even though the peptide can be attached to the GOQD, fluorescence recovery can still be attained due to the stronger affinity between peptide and melamine.

In the case of Melp4, Melp1-2, Melp2-2, Melp3-2, and Melp5-2, however, there are no changes according to the melamine concentration (Fig. S10). To determine the selectivity of the discovered peptides, various chemicals carrying aromatic rings were implemented in the system. As a control group, toluene, phenol, xylene, and o-dichlorobenzene were selected, and the results for each peptide are shown in Fig. 5 . The fluorescence changes for each chemical were normalized, and other chemicals had low recovery values, except for melamine. Interestingly, both Melp2 and Melp3 having similar sequences show the low fluorescence responses to the control group. Consequently, it was revealed that these sequences have high affinity and selectivity toward melamine.

The designed assay methods for melamine were tested on melamine-spiked skim milk samples. Since melamine is not spontaneously generated from the dairy products but intentionally added by food fraud behavior, thus, this kind of samples can represent a real sample. As the proteins included in the skim milk could affect the fluorescence response, the samples were pretreated via TCA precipitation before analysis to remove proteins. The melamine-detecting performance was somewhat reduced owing to the remaining salt and proteins; whole proved peptides produce increased fluorescence intensity according to the amount of melamine (Fig. S11).

Since the electrochemical sensor for melamine is intended for one-time use by SPE, not only repeatability and stability but also sensitivity of the bioreceptor are of prominent criteria to pick up the best hit among the candidates. Based on the validation test, Melp2 exhibited low error bar and LOD as well as high linearity even in the skim milk sample. Hence, it was applied for the electrochemical assay (Fig. 6 A). As an effective technique to represent the actual condition of the electrode surface, EIS technique was adopted for this research.

To figure out the optimal concentration of peptide, Au-SPE was incubated with different concentrations of Melp2 (0.05, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, and 1.0 mg/mL) then recorded their EIS. As shown in Fig. S12A, the semicircle diameter of each result is the electron transfer resistance (Rct), and the value is highly dependent on the dielectric and insulating features at the electrode/electrolyte interface [46,47]. While the Rct was elevated up to 0.3 mg/mL due to the insulation effect of the peptide, a steady value slightly lower than that point was observed (Fig. 6B) [48]. When a high concentration of peptide was treated on the electrode, the excessed things could be stacked on the surface of the electrode as a form of agglomerates and decrease the impedance value [49]. For these reasons, 0.3 mg/mL of Melp2 was chosen for the adequate concentration for the electrochemical sensing of melamine.

To implement the electrochemical assay of melamine using the optimized concentration of Melp2 peptide, the peptide-modified Au-SPE was reacted with the various concentrations of melamine then recorded their EIS spectra. As shown in Fig. S12B, the semicircle diameter was raised according to the melamine amount due to its poor electrochemical activity [50]. The linear regression equation for the sensor was Rct (Ω) = 1.75 × [Melamine] + 0.75 (R2 = 0.9954; n = 3), and the LOD was 6.89 ppb, which is calculated by 3σ-rule (Fig. 6C). Whereas the peptide-modified SPE reflects the concentration of melamine as the impedance, there were no changes of Rct when just reacted melamine to the SPE without peptide treatment (Fig. S13).

In order to evaluate the selectivity, possible interferents commonly present in milk (e.g. KCl, CaCl2, ascorbic acid, leucine, lysine, proline, and valine) were applied to the system [51]. As shown in Figs. 6D and S12C, less than 15 % of signal increment was observed compared to the impedance value of melamine. Moreover, the impedance value of the sensor was also increased as the concentration of melamine in the case of melamine-spiked skim milk samples (Figs. 6E and S12D). The linear regression equation was Rct (Ω) = 1.51 × [Melamine] + 0.82 (R2 = 0.9953; n = 3), and the LOD was 21.26 ppb. The assay performance of the system is slightly lowered in the skim milk sample, but the proposed peptide-based impedimetric biosensor demonstrates high sensitivity and selectivity, comparable to other works as presented in Table S4. Based on the linear regression curve of the skim milk results, the recovery test of melamine in liquid milk was performed. To prepare the samples, liquid milk was brought from a mart then pretreated by the same way for the skim milk sample preparation. The melamine in the liquid milk sample was measured using standard addition methods, in which the amount was 50, 100, and 1000 ppb, respectively, as shown in Table S5. The recovery range was 99.1%–106.4% with a low relative standard deviation (less than 8.4 %). To examine the stability of the proposed technique, the sensor was identically prepared on the same day, and then relative impedance change is measured every 5 days after under 1 ppm of melamine. After 15 days, the activity of the sensor is retained by more than 97 %, with high repeatability, even it was stored at room temperature (Fig. S14). These results show that the discovered peptides exhibited high sensitivity and stability and have excellent practical feasibility for the development of an impedimetric sensor toward melamine with lasting stability.

In summary, we developed melamine-specific peptides using phage display technology and constructed the peptide verification methods via the melamine-mediated GOQD-Hg2+ quenching system. To proceed this experiment, melamine was haptenized and applied to two different phage display experiments to enhance their specificity and selectivity by cross-checking the results. After all discovered peptide sequences were investigated, five analogs and repeated sequences were selected for the candidates. These peptides were validated by GOQD-Hg2+ quenching system, and it was exhibited that they all had a high binding affinity to melamine except Melp4. When this system was applied to the melamine sensors, Melp2 shows up to 3.12 ppm of LOD for the spiked samples, and it was still worked for 9.55 ppm of LOD even in pretreated spiked skim milk samples. Furthermore, Mepl2 was applied for the electrochemical sensor, it represents the 6.89 ppb and 21.26 ppb of LOD for spiked sample and pretreated spiked skim milk samples, respectively. In consequence, the validity of the sequence was validified by various ways, and we expect that these sequences could be utilized for the development of various types of sensors for melamine.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chan Yeong Park received his M.S. degree in 2017 from Chung-Ang University, South Korea. Currently, he received his Ph.D. degree in the Department of Chemistry, Chung-Ang University, South Korea. His research interests mainly focus on the synthesis of nanomaterials, drug-delivery system, and bioimaging.

Min Woo Kim received his B.S. degree in 2017 from Yonsei University of chemistry department, South Korea. Currently, he is an M.S. student at the Department of chemistry, Chung-Ang University. His research interests focus on the nanomaterials, development of nanoparticles-based detection sensors for biomolecules and viruses.
Kyoung Yeol Park received M.S. degree in 2019 from Chun-Ang University, South Korea. His research interests mainly focus on the in vitro bioassay.

Seung Hoon Beak received his B.S. degree in Nanochemistry from Sunmoon University in 2014. Now, he received his Ph.D. degree in Chung-Ang University in 2020. His research interests focus on the synthesis of nanomaterials and applications for the detection of environmental contaminants and biomaterials.

Ruth Stephanie received her Bachelor's degree in Materials Engineering department from Bandung Institute of Technology, Bandung, Indonesia in 2020. Currently, she is on her Master's course at the Department of Chemistry, Chung-Ang University, Seoul, Korea. Her research interest is the synthesis of nanomaterials and nanocomposites to be applied for biosensing purposes.

Subhadeep Ghosh received his Master’s degree in Applied Chemistry department from S. V National Institute of Technology, Surat, India 2018, Currently he is pursuing Ph.D. at Chung-Ang University, Seoul, Korea. His research interest includes the design and synthesis of novel nanomaterials on the platform of fluorescent-based detection and imaging.

Prof. Hyang Sook Chun Prof. Dr. Hyang Sook Chun received a Ph.D. degree from the Department of Food Science and Nutrition, Ewha Womans University, Korea. She has been a principal research scientist for 20 years at the Korea Food Research Institute. She is currently a professor of the Department of Food Science and Technology, Chung-Ang University. Her research focuses on the toxicity, analysis, and risk assessment of natural toxins such as mycotoxin. In addition, as a PI of the Food Fraud Research Group, recent research has focused on identifying a variety of food frauds and developing analytical methods for food authenticity. She has received the Order of Science and Technological Merit from the Korean government, published more than 180 research papers, and holds more than 60 international and Korean patents.

Prof. Yun Suk Huh received his Ph.D degree in the Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, KAIST, Daejeon, Republic of Korea in 2007. Now he is Associate Professor in the Department of Biological Engineering at Inha University, Incheon, Republic of Korea. His current research interests are to develop the optical and electrochemical sensors for the highly sensitive detection of biomolecules and diseases. In addition, he is working on the synthesis of bifunctional materials for drug delivery and therapy. He has published more than 100 research articles in SCI journals.

Prof. Jang-Kyo Kim graduated with B.Eng. in Aeronautical Engineering from Seoul National University; M.Eng.Sci. in Materials Engineering from Monash University; and PhD in composite materials from Department of Mechanical Engineering, University of Sydney. He was an ARC Postdoctoral Research Fellow (1992–1993), then started his faculty career at Australian National University (1993–1994) before joining the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (HKUST) in 1994. He had visiting appointments with University of Tokyo and Nanyang Technological University on a Tan Chin Tuan Exchange Fellowship (1998); and has been a Visiting Professor at Center for Advanced Materials Technology, University of Sydney since 2012. He is currently a Chair Professor at HKUST and directs the Finetex-HKUST R & D Center and the Advanced Engineering Materials Facilities. He served as Associate Dean of Engineering (2007–2011). His research is focused on advanced materials, ranging from fiber-reinforced composites to nanocomposites with graphene, carbon nanotubes, nanoclay and 2D materials, and to nanostructured materials for various energy storage applications. He has written well over 350 refereed journal papers, 12 book chapters, 15 books including three authored research monographs, and holds 12 international patents. His research team has been honored with 12 Best Paper Awards from international conferences and competitions. Currently, Prof. Kim holds editorial positions for a number of journals as Editor (Composites Part A), Associate Editor (Aerospace Science and Technology) and Editorial Board Members (Energy Storage Materials, Nanoscale Horizons, etc.). He served as the President of the Asian-Australasian Association of Composites Materials (AACM) (2008–2010); and is elected Fellows of the Hong Kong Academy of Engineering Sciences (HKAES), the Royal Aeronautical Society (RAeS), the Royal Society of Chemistry (RSC) and the Hong Kong Institution of Engineers (HKIE). He is a Highly Cited Researcher in Materials Science by Clarivate Analytics (Web of Science).

Prof. Jong Pil Park obtained his Ph.D. at Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering in Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST). He was post-doc with Annelise E. Barron (Northwestern University, 2005−2008. USA) and Scott Banta (Columbia University, 2008–2009, USA). Since September 2009, he appointed as Assistant Professor of Pharmaceutical Engineering at Daegu Haany University and currently moved to Chung-Ang University in 2020. He is now an Associate Professor at Department of Food Science and Technology, Chung-Ang University. His group works on the development of new affinity reagents over antibodies for the use of biosensor, the identification and analysis of novel affinity peptides, the engineering of peptide-based molecular binder and novel approaches to functional biosensing platform and artificial scaffold. He has published over 100 peer-reviewed publications and 4 book chapters and has served as Editor of Biotechnology and Bioprocess Engineering.

Prof. Dr. Tae Jung Park received his Ph.D. degree at the Department of Chemical & Biomolecular Engineering of KAIST in 2004. He has been a professor with a distinguished scholarship at the Chemistry department of Chung-Ang University. His research interests are novel platform technologies for nanobio-fusion studies on metal surfaces, biosensors, and nanocomplex fabrication for electrochemical and optical analyses. Furthermore, he is interested in the investigation of molecular- and immune-diagnostics using nanomaterials and the evaluation of biomimetics and drug delivery systems. He has received over 20 research awards, published over 210 papers and 5 book chapters, and holds over 50 international and 100 Korean patents.
